prompt.task: Write a long-form content piece that dives deep into the topic below. The piece should be rigorous, coherent, and concrete.
prompt.topic: Microservices Architecture: When Monoliths Make More Sense
prompt.audience: Technical leaders and senior engineers evaluating architecture decisions

source_policy.policy: Draw on well-known industry examples (Netflix, Amazon, Segment's return to monolith). Present these as illustrative cases, not definitive proof.
Distinguish between theoretical benefits and observed outcomes in practice.

constraints.constraint: Start by defining both microservices and monoliths in plain language.
Use the "Total Cost of Ownership" framework to organize trade-offs.
Include both team-size and technical complexity as decision factors.
Provide a concrete decision tree or rubric for choosing between approaches.
Introduce key terms (service mesh, eventual consistency, etc.) before using them.
Distinguish between: (a) established facts, (b) reasonable inference, (c) speculation.
Avoid name-dropping; only cite concepts/people if they add explanatory value.
Use the exact section headings specified in output_contract.
No preamble, no meta-commentary about the prompt.
Keep paragraphs reasonably short; prefer clarity over density.

prompt.optional_context: Focus on organizations with 5-50 engineers, where the decision is most consequential. The reader is likely facing pressure to adopt microservices and needs ammunition for a nuanced conversation with stakeholders.

reader_promises.promise: The reader can articulate specific conditions where monoliths outperform microservices.
The reader can identify warning signs of premature microservice adoption.
The reader has a framework for making the decision in their own context.

output_contract.format: Use markdown with H2 section headings exactly as written below. Under each heading, write substantive content (not bullet-only sections).

required_headings.heading: Introduction and explanation
Technique deep dive
Pros and cons, when and where to use
Use cases and examples
Applicability and potential
What's next

requirements.item: Define microservices and monoliths without jargon
Explain why this decision matters (cost, velocity, reliability)
Preview the Total Cost of Ownership framework
Break down operational costs: deployment, monitoring, debugging
Walk through a realistic migration scenario end-to-end
Team size thresholds (when coordination cost exceeds benefit)
Technical complexity thresholds (when distribution adds more than it solves)
Example 1: Successful microservices (what made it work)
Example 2: Failed migration / return to monolith (what went wrong)
Edge case: The modular monolith as middle ground
Decision rubric: 5 questions to answer before choosing
Where monolith-first is the dominant strategy
What capabilities microservices unlock that can't be achieved otherwise
Emerging patterns: modular monoliths, serverless, cell-based architecture
Next steps for a team currently on a monolith
Open questions: how AI-assisted development changes the calculus

output_contract.length: Aim for 2500-3500 words. Prefer completeness over brevity, but avoid filler.
